summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the fluid mosaic model, and who proposed it?

The fluid mosaic model (Singer & Nicolson, 1972): (1) 'Fluid' = phospholipids and most proteins are not fixed in place — they move laterally within the plane of the membrane (lateral diffusion). Demonstrated by Frye & Edidin (1970) cell fusion experiment: fluorescently labeled human and mouse membrane proteins intermixed within 40 minutes. (2) 'Mosaic' = the membrane contains a diverse array of proteins (integral/transmembrane proteins that span the bilayer, peripheral proteins attached to surfaces) and other components (cholesterol, glycolipids, glycoproteins) creating a mosaic pattern. (3) Replaced earlier models: Davson-Danielli 'sandwich' model (1935) — proposed protein coated both surfaces of a lipid bilayer. Robertson's unit membrane model (1960s) — proposed all biological membranes were identical. (4) Key features: asymmetry (inner and outer leaflets differ in lipid and protein composition), fluidity (varies with temperature, fatty acid saturation, cholesterol content), and selectivity (controls what enters/exits the cell).

What is the structure of a phospholipid, and why does its amphipathic nature drive membrane formation?

Phospholipid structure and membrane formation: (1) Structure: glycerol backbone with two fatty acid tails (hydrophobic, typically 16-18 carbons, one often unsaturated with a cis double bond creating a kink) and one phosphate head group (hydrophilic, often modified with choline, ethanolamine, serine, or inositol → phosphatidylcholine, phosphatidylethanolamine, phosphatidylserine, phosphatidylinositol). (2) Amphipathic = having both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions. (3) Self-assembly: in aqueous solution, phospholipids spontaneously form bilayers to minimize hydrophobic exposure to water — thermodynamically favorable (increases entropy of surrounding water). Can also form micelles (single layer, tails inward) or liposomes (bilayer spheres). (4) Bilayer properties: ~5 nm thick. Hydrophobic core prevents passage of ions and polar molecules. Flexible — can bend, fuse, and bud. Self-sealing — if punctured, phospholipids rearrange to close the gap.

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

HIV entry and membrane proteins: (1) HIV entry steps: gp120 (viral surface glycoprotein) binds CD4 receptor (integral membrane protein on T-helper cells, macrophages, dendritic cells) → conformational change in gp120 exposes co-receptor binding site → gp120 binds CCR5 or CXCR4 (chemokine receptors, 7-transmembrane GPCRs) → conformational change in gp41 (viral transmembrane protein) → fusion peptide of gp41 inserts into host membrane → viral and host membranes fuse → viral capsid enters cell. (2) CCR5-Δ32 mutation: 32 base pair deletion in CCR5 gene → premature stop codon → truncated protein not expressed on cell surface. Homozygotes (Δ32/Δ32, ~1% of European descent): highly resistant to R5-tropic HIV strains. Heterozygotes: slower disease progression. (3) Clinical application: maraviroc (CCR5 antagonist drug) blocks the co-receptor. Timothy Brown ('Berlin patient') — received bone marrow transplant from CCR5-Δ32 homozygous donor → functionally cured of HIV. (4) Demonstrates: how specific integral membrane proteins serve as viral receptors, and how membrane protein mutations can confer disease resistance.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A pharmaceutical company is developing a new drug delivery system using liposomes — artificial phospholipid bilayer vesicles that can encapsulate medications. How does the amphipathic nature of phospholipids make this technology possible, and what advantages do liposomes offer?

Liposome drug delivery — applied membrane biology: (1) Self-assembly: amphipathic phospholipids spontaneously form bilayer vesicles (liposomes, 50 nm-5 μm diameter) when dispersed in aqueous solution. Thermodynamically driven to minimize hydrophobic exposure to water. (2) Drug loading: hydrophilic drugs (e.g., doxorubicin for cancer — Doxil®) in aqueous interior; hydrophobic drugs (e.g., amphotericin B for fungal infections — AmBisome®) within lipid bilayer; amphipathic drugs at the bilayer-water interface. (3) Advantages: biocompatible and biodegradable; reduce systemic toxicity (cancer drugs reach tumor preferentially via EPR effect — enhanced permeability and retention); protect drugs from degradation; can be modified with PEG (stealth liposomes — avoid immune clearance) or targeting ligands (antibodies, peptides) for cell-specific delivery. (4) mRNA vaccines (Pfizer/Moderna COVID-19): use lipid nanoparticles (related to liposomes) to deliver mRNA into cells — the lipid bilayer protects fragile mRNA and facilitates cellular uptake via endocytosis.
